text: Direct electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water-oxidation intermediates has emerged as a potentially safer and more sustainable approach compared to chlorine or peroxide based processes. Here we show that an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst PdPtO x /C with an average Pd/Pt ratio of 2 yields a faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% toward selective propylene oxidation at a current density of 50 mA cm −2 at ambient pressure and temperature. Enhanced catalytic performance was realized by the incorporation of platinum into the palladium oxide structure, resulting in the formation of stabilized oxidized platinum species. Catalyst kinetics analysis indicates that propylene oxidation on PdPtO x /C proceeds via a metal-centered electrophilic mechanism that involves the interaction of propylene with metal-bound peroxo species derived from water oxidation. Our study presents a catalyst system that achieves selective electrochemical oxygen-atom transfer to substrates from water media without the need of redox-active catalyst mediators.
